answer: The mean action value accounts for in-flight parallel descents by incorporating virtual loss into the effective value and visit count used to compute Q, where each in-flight virtual visit is treated as a -1 outcome from the parent's perspective, discouraging multiple threads from exploring the same edge simultaneously. [S6]

The mean action value accounts for in-flight parallel descents by treating each virtual loss as a -1 outcome from the parent's perspective, which reduces the effective value and increases the effective visit count in the Q calculation, thereby discouraging multiple threads from exploring the same edge concurrently. [S2] [S1]

The MCTS player decides to keep part of the previous search tree when the opponent has made exactly one move (delta-1 case) by descending through `game.LastAction()` to find a matching child in `saved_root_` and using it as the new root, provided the child is not terminal and has children. A candidate subtree is disqualified if the matched child is a visited-but-unexpanded leaf (no children), as promoting it to root would leave `Select` with nothing to choose, causing a fall-through to the fresh-build path. Terminal positions are handled separately by the outer search loop and do not disqualify reuse. [S1] [S3] [S9]

When the same MCTS player is queried again after the opponent has made exactly one move (delta-1 case), it attempts to reuse part of the previous search tree by descending through `game.LastAction()` to find a matching child in `saved_root_` and using that child as the new root, provided it is not terminal and has children. A candidate subtree is disqualified if the matched child is a visited-but-unexpanded leaf (no children), as promoting it to root would leave `Select` with nothing to choose, causing the player to fall through to the fresh-build path. Terminal positions are handled separately by the outer search loop and do not disqualify reuse in this context. [S1] [S3] [S9]

When the same MCTS player is queried again after the opponent has made exactly one move (delta-1 case), it descends through `game.LastAction()` to the matching child and clears its parent, reusing that subtree as the new root if it is not a terminal node and has children. [S3] A candidate subtree is disqualified if the matched child is a visited-but-unexpanded leaf (no children), as promoting it to root would leave `Select` with nothing to choose, causing a fall-through to the fresh-build path. [S3] Terminal positions are also handled separately and do not qualify for reuse in this context. [S3] Unmatched siblings remain in the arena as dead weight until the next end-of-search migration sweeps them out. [S3]

The mean action value accounts for in-flight parallel descents by treating each virtual loss as a -1 outcome from the parent's perspective, which reduces the effective value and increases the effective visit count in the Q calculation, discouraging multiple threads from exploring the same edge simultaneously. [S2] The formula used is effective_visits = visits + virtual_loss, effective_value = total_value - virtual_loss, and Q = effective_value / effective_visits, as demonstrated in the test where a child with VL=1 and no real visits yields Q = -1, and with two real wins (Q=+1) and VL=1 yields Q = (2 - 1) / 3. [S1] This mechanism is implemented via AddVirtualLoss and RemoveVirtualLoss methods that atomically adjust the virtual_loss_ counter. [S3] Sequential search leaves virtual_loss = 0 throughout, but concurrent drivers use this API to penalize in-flight visits. [S2]

The mean action value accounts for in-flight parallel descents by treating each virtual loss as a -1 outcome from the parent's perspective, which reduces the effective value and increases the effective visit count in the Q calculation, thereby discouraging multiple threads from exploring the same edge simultaneously. [S2] The formula used is effective_visits = visits + virtual_loss, effective_value = total_value - virtual_loss, and Q = effective_value / effective_visits, as demonstrated in the test where a child with VL=1 and no real visits yields Q = -1, and with two real wins (Q=+1) and one VL yields Q = (2 - 1) / 3. [S1] This mechanism is implemented via AddVirtualLoss and RemoveVirtualLoss methods that atomically adjust the virtual_loss_ counter. [S3] Sequential search leaves virtual_loss = 0 throughout, but concurrent drivers use it to penalize in-flight visits. [S2]

The MCTS player can keep part of the previous search tree when the opponent has made exactly one move (delta-1) by descending through `game.LastAction()` to the matching child and clearing its parent, provided the matched child is not terminal and has children. [S1] A candidate subtree is disqualified if the matched child is a visited-but-unexpanded leaf (no children), as promoting it to root would leave `Select` with nothing to choose, causing a fall-through to the fresh-build path. [S1] Terminal positions are handled separately by the outer search loop and do not disqualify reuse on their own, but a non-terminal match must have children to qualify. [S1] [S3] [S4] The condition for reuse is explicitly checked via `if (match && !match->IsTerminal() && !match->Children().empty())`. [S3] [S4] If this condition fails, the player resets saved state and builds a fresh arena. [S4]

The batched inference server decides between a single-buffered synchronous path and a two-deep pipelined path based on whether the model implements `IAsyncForwardModel`; if it does (as with `CudaTransformerFp16` and `CudaTransformerFp8`), the executor uses two pinned slots and pipelines submit/wait two-deep, while models that only implement the synchronous `IModel` surface (such as `CudaMlp`, `SplitCudaMlp`, and FP32 `CudaTransformer`) use a single pinned slot and the original sync path. The executor publishes a coherent set of mirrored weights to in-flight forward passes by using a seqlock-style version counter (`mirror_version_`); it only flips the version after `Mirror()` completes, ensuring that any in-flight `ForwardBatch` sees a fully-coherent set of parameters. [S1] [S3] [S4] [S8] [S11] [S16]

The batched inference server decides to use a two-deep pipelined path when the model implements `IAsyncForwardModel` (such as `CudaTransformerFp16` or `CudaTransformerFp8`), using two pinned slots to overlap CPU packing with GPU execution; otherwise, for models that only implement the synchronous `IModel` interface (such as `CudaMlp`, `SplitCudaMlp`, FP32 `CudaTransformer`, or `SplitCudaTransformer`), it uses a single-buffered synchronous path with one pinned slot. [S3] [S4] [S8]  
The `Mirror()` method publishes a coherent set of mirrored weights to in-flight forward passes by copying parameters from the trainer's write-side model into the server's read-side model between batches, using a seqlock-style version counter (`mirror_version_`) that is only flipped after `Mirror()` completes, ensuring any in-flight `ForwardBatch` sees a fully consistent set of weights. [S1] [S13] [S5]

The squared-gradient sum is accumulated across all parameter buffers by launching sum_sq_kernel for each gradient buffer (weights and biases) with a shared device memory double* d_sum_sq that is zeroed before the kernels and reduced via atomicAdd<double> [S2][S3]. The L2 weight-decay term is never affected by the clip ratio because the grad_scale multiplies the data gradient before the weight-decay term is added in the adam_kernel and sgd_momentum_kernel, ensuring weight decay is applied to the unscaled parameters [S8][S9][S10][S5].

The batched inference server decides between a single-buffered synchronous path and a two-deep pipelined path based on whether the model implements `IAsyncForwardModel`; if it does (e.g., `CudaTransformerFp16` / `CudaTransformerFp8`), the executor uses two pinned slots and pipelines submit/wait two-deep, otherwise it uses a single pinned slot and the original sync path [S2]. The executor publishes a coherent set of mirrored weights to in-flight forward passes by flipping the `mirror_version_` seqlock-style counter only after `Mirror()` completes, ensuring any in-flight `ForwardBatch` sees a fully-coherent set of parameters [S3].

The batched inference server decides between a single-buffered synchronous path and a two-deep pipelined path based on whether the model implements `IAsyncForwardModel`; if it does (as with FP16/FP8 transformer replicas), the server uses two pinned slots and pipelines submit/wait two-deep, otherwise it uses a single pinned slot and the synchronous `ForwardBatch` path [S4]. The executor publishes a coherent set of mirrored weights to in-flight forward passes by draining in-flight async slots (calling `WaitForward` and resolving pending requests) before running `MirrorParametersFrom`, ensuring readers never observe partially copied parameters [S5]. The seqlock-style version counter (`mirror_version_`) is flipped only after `Mirror()` completes, so any in-flight `ForwardBatch` sees a fully-coherent set of parameters [S8].

The cache variable is `AZ_GAME_XQ_GIT_TAG` and the pinned tag is `"v0.2.7"`; the unit-test build is gated by the `AZ_BUILD_TESTS` option, which defaults to OFF and enables testing and adds the `tests` subdirectory when ON. [S5] [S4]

The global-L2-norm gradient clipping is arranged so that the L2 weight-decay term is never affected by the clip ratio by scaling the data gradient with `grad_scale` before adding the weight-decay term in the Adam and SGD momentum kernels, as shown in `adam_kernel` where `g = grad_scale * grads[i] + weight_decay * params[i]` and similarly in `sgd_momentum_kernel` (implied by shared interface). [S12] The squared-gradient sum is accumulated across all parameter buffers by launching `sum_sq_kernel` for each parameter blob's gradient buffer, using atomicAdd<double> to sum squares into a single device memory location `d_grad_sum_sq_`, which is zeroed before each accumulation and later copied to host for norm computation. [S9] [S14] [S11] [S4] [S6] [S8] [S3] [S2] [S1] [S5] [S7] [S10] [S13]

For the FP32 trainer, the tensor-core (WMMA) forward kernel is used for head dimensions `d_h ∈ {16, 32}` and the scalar kernel is used for `d_h ∈ {4, 8, 64}`. [S8] The larger head dimension `d_h=64` is excluded because its shared memory footprint (~96 KB) exceeds the per-CTA static capacity cap, making it ineligible for the WMMA path without opting into dynamic shared memory, which is not used for the FP32 trainer's WMMA forward kernel. [S8] [S3] [S1]

The global-L2-norm gradient clipping is arranged by first accumulating the squared-gradient sum across all parameter buffers using the `sum_sq_kernel` with `atomicAdd<double>` on a single device memory location, then computing the clip ratio on the host and passing it as `grad_scale` to the optimizer kernels. The squared-gradient sum is accumulated by launching `sum_sq_kernel` for each gradient buffer (e.g., `d_train_dw1_`, `d_train_db1_`, etc.) targeting the same `d_sum_sq` output, with the caller responsible for zeroing it before launch. In the Adam and SGD update kernels, the `grad_scale` multiplies only the data gradient term (`grad_scale * grads[i]`), while the weight-decay term (`weight_decay * params[i]`) is added separately, ensuring the L2 weight-decay term is never affected by the clip ratio. [S3][S4][S8][S9][S10][S11][S12][S13][S14][S15]

The cache variable is `AZ_GAME_XQ_GIT_TAG` and the pinned tag is `"v0.2.7"`; the unit-test build is gated by the `AZ_BUILD_TESTS` option, which defaults to OFF and enables testing and adds the `tests` subdirectory when ON. [S5] [S4]

The cache variable is `AZ_GAME_XQ_GIT_TAG` and the pinned tag is `"v0.2.7"`; the unit-test build is gated by the `AZ_BUILD_TESTS` option, which defaults to OFF and enables testing and adds the `tests` subdirectory when ON. [S4][S6]

CUBLAS_COMPUTE_32F_FAST_TF32 makes the FP32 trainer GEMMs run on tensor cores [S2]. The FP8 entry point that produces FP32 output does not fuse the bias in the epilogue because cuBLASLt requires bias dtype to match output dtype and the replica only mirrors FP16 biases [S3].

CUBLAS_COMPUTE_32F_FAST_TF32 makes the FP32 trainer GEMMs run on tensor cores [S2]. The FP8 entry point that produces FP32 output does not fuse the bias in the epilogue because cuBLASLt requires bias dtype to match output dtype and the replica only mirrors FP16 biases [S3].

CUBLAS_COMPUTE_32F_FAST_TF32 makes the FP32 trainer GEMMs run on tensor cores [S3]. The FP8 entry point that produces FP32 output does not fuse the bias in the epilogue because cuBLASLt requires bias dtype to match output dtype and the replica only mirrors FP16 biases [S2].

The compute type that makes the FP32 trainer GEMMs run on tensor cores is `CUBLAS_COMPUTE_32F_FAST_TF32`, as used in the FP32+TF32 entry points for the trainer. [S2] [S8]  
The FP8 entry point that produces FP32 output (`RunFp8To32`) does not fuse the bias in the epilogue because cuBLASLt requires the bias dtype to match the output dtype, and the replica biases are kept as FP16, so the bias is added separately by the caller. [S12] [S13] [S10]

The tensor-core (WMMA) forward kernel is used for head dimensions kDh ∈ {16, 32} in the FP32 fused-attention implementation, as determined by the constexpr function FlashAttnFp32UseWmma<kDh>() which returns true only for these values [S1]. The larger head dimension kDh=64 is excluded because its shared memory footprint (~96 KB) exceeds the per-CTA opt-in cap, making it fall back to the scalar one-thread-per-row kernel [S1]. This is also reflected in the launch logic where the WMMA kernel is conditionally instantiated based on FlashAttnFp32UseWmma<kDh>() [S3]. The FP32 trainer uses TF32 WMMA forward specifically for d_h ∈ {16, 32}, while backward remains scalar after WMMA dQ was measured to be slower [S5]. The WMMA path requires kDh % 8 == 0 for TF32 m16n16k8 compatibility, but only kDh=16 and 32 are selected due to shared memory constraints [S1]. For kDh=64, the SMEM footprint exceeds the 48 KB static cap, necessitating a fallback to scalar kernels despite meeting the divisibility requirement [S1]. The test utility UsesWmma(int d_h) confirms that only d_h == 16 || d_h == 32 returns true, aligning with the WMMA usage condition [S4]. Thus, the WMMA forward kernel is used exclusively for head dimensions 16 and 32 in FP32 mode, with 64 excluded due to excessive shared memory usage. [S1][S3][S5][S4]

The transformer is split between layers: the first n_layers / 2 layers live on device A, the rest on device B, with the embedding on device A and the final norm + head on device B. [S1] [S2] [S4] The boundary traffic per forward pass is one [B, T, d_model] BF16 tensor in each direction. [S1] At chess scale (B=256, T=64, d_model=512), this is 16 MB per direction. [S1]

The global-L2-norm gradient clipping is arranged by first accumulating the squared-gradient sum across all parameter buffers using the `sum_sq_kernel` with `atomicAdd<double>` to compute `sum(g[i]^2)` into a single device memory location, which is then copied to the host to compute the total norm and derive `grad_scale` as `grad_clip_norm / total_norm` if clipping is needed [S2][S3]. The squared-gradient sum is accumulated across all parameter buffers by launching `sum_sq_kernel` for each gradient buffer (e.g., `d_train_dw1_`, `d_train_db1_`, etc.) and using the same `d_sum_sq` pointer for all launches, allowing atomic addition to a shared double [S2][S3]. The L2 weight-decay term is never affected by the clip ratio because the `grad_scale` argument multiplies only the data gradient (`grads[i]`) in the update kernels, while the weight-decay term (`weight_decay * params[i]`) is added separately and unscaled, as seen in the Adam kernel where the gradient update is `grad_scale * grads[i] + weight_decay * params[i]` [S8]. This ensures the weight-decay term is not scaled by `grad_scale`, preserving its independence from gradient clipping [S5]. The same principle applies to the SGD momentum kernel, which also takes `grad_scale` and applies it only to the gradient term [S9][S10][S12][S13]. The transformer backend follows the same pattern, using `ComputeGradientL2Norm_()` to compute the norm and applying `grad_scale` in `ApplyOptimizer_()` [S7][S11]. The `sum_sq_kernel` uses a two-stage reduction: first, each thread computes a local sum of squares, then a block-level reduction via shared memory, and finally, thread 0 atomically adds the block sum to the global output [S1][S4]. The caller is responsible for zeroing `*out` before launching across all parameter gradient buffers [S1]. The host-side reduction is a single `cudaMemcpy` to retrieve the accumulated sum [S1]. The `sum_sq_kernel` requires `sm_60+` for `atomicAdd<double>`, which is supported by all targeted devices [S1]. The gradient clipping is disabled when `grad_clip_norm <= 0.

AZ_GAME_XQ_GIT_TAG and AZ_GAME_XQ_SOURCE_DIR select the concrete game; unit-test build is gated by AZ_BUILD_TESTS option. [S5] shows AZ_GAME_XQ_GIT_TAG and AZ_GAME_XQ_SOURCE_DIR as cache variables for az-game-xiang-qi, and [S4] shows the unit-test build gated by option(AZ_BUILD_TESTS "Build engine unit tests" OFF) and if(AZ_BUILD_TESTS). [S1] confirms the concrete game is swapped in at configure time via CMake FetchContent and the currently-wired game is az-game-xiang-qi pinned to tag v0.2.1 (though S5 shows v0.2.7, the mechanism is the same). [S3] explains editing CMakeLists.txt to replace the FetchContent_Declare block and rename cache vars. [S4] and [S5] together show the cache variables and the test gating. [S11] shows the FetchContent_MakeAvailable call including both game repos, but only XiangQiGame is linked into alphazero_* executables per [S1] and [S3]. The unit-test build is explicitly controlled by AZ_BUILD_TESTS as shown in [S4] and [S6].
I don't know from the provided sources.

The transformer is split between layers: the first n_layers / 2 layers live on device A, and the remaining layers on device B, with the embedding on device A and the final norm + head on device B. [S1] [S2] [S4] The boundary traffic per forward pass is one [B, T, d_model] BF16 tensor in each direction. [S1] At chess scale (B=256, T=64, d_model=512), this is 16 MB per direction. [S1]

The measured slowdown was ~3.7× relative to the old board-only shape [S1]. The optimization that recovered most of the loss was FlashAttention, which improved throughput by 2.14× on the new shape [S2].

The transformer is split between layers: the first n_layers / 2 layers live on device A, the rest on device B, with the embedding on device A and the final norm + head on device B. [S1] The seam is one [B, T, d_model] BF16 tensor in each direction per forward pass. [S1] At chess scale (B=256, T=64, d_model=512), this is 16 MB per direction. [S1]

The tensor-core (WMMA) forward kernel is used for head dimensions kDh ∈ {16, 32} in the FP32 fused-attention implementation, as determined by the constexpr function FlashAttnFp32UseWmma<kDh>() which returns true only for these values [S1]. The larger head dimension kDh=64 is excluded because its shared memory footprint (~96 KB) exceeds the per-CTA opt-in cap, making it fall back to the scalar one-thread-per-row kernel [S1]. This is also reflected in the launch logic where the WMMA kernel is conditionally instantiated based on FlashAttnFp32UseWmma<kDh>() [S3]. The FP16 inference replica uses WMMA for d_h ∈ {16, 32, 64} with dynamic shared memory for d_h=64, but the FP32 trainer restricts WMMA to {16, 32} due to SMEM constraints [S2][S5]. The test utility UsesWmma confirms that only d_h=16 and d_h=32 trigger the WMMA path [S4]. The backward kernel remains scalar after WMMA dQ was measured slower [S2][S5]. The TF32 WMMA path requires kDh % 8 == 0, but the SMEM limit further restricts usable dimensions to {16, 32} [S1]. The documentation states that the FP32 trainer uses TF32 WMMA forward at d_h ∈ {16, 32} while backward stays scalar [S2]. The FP16 WMMA forward is the production path for d_h ∈ {16, 32, 64}, but the FP32 trainer does not use it for d_h=64 due to SMEM exceeding the 48 KB static cap [S2]. The WMMA forward kernel for FP32 uses TF32 m16n16k8 and requires kDh multiple of 8, but the SMEM footprint at kDh=64 (~96 KB) exceeds the per-CTA opt-in cap, so only kDh ∈ {16, 32} are used [S1]. The tensor-core (WMMA) forward kernel is used for head dimensions 16 and 32 in the FP32 fused-attention implementation, as determined by the condition `kDh == 16 || kDh == 32` in the `FlashAttnFp32UseWmma` function. [S1] The larger head dimension of 64 is excluded because the shared memory footprint at `kDh=64` (~96 KB) exceeds the per-CTA opt-in cap, making it fall back to the scalar one-thread-per-row kernel. [S1] This is further supported by the statement that `kDh ∈ {4, 8, 64}` fall back to the scalar one-thread-per-row kernels. [S1] The WMMA forward kernel is only instantiated for `kDh` values where TF32 m16n16k8 requires `kDh % 8 == 0`, but the shared memory constraint limits usage to 16 and 32. [S1] The FP32 trainer uses TF32 WMMA forward at `d_h ∈ {16, 32}`, while backward stays scalar after WMMA dQ measured slower. [S2] The launcher conditionally invokes the WMMA kernel only when `FlashAttnFp32UseWmma<kDh>()` returns true, which is exclusively for `kDh == 16 || kDh == 32`. [S3] The test utility `UsesWmma` confirms this behavior, returning true only for `d_h == 16 || d_h == 32`. [S4] Documentation notes that the FP16 WMMA forward is the production path for `d_h ∈ {16, 32, 64}`, but the FP32 trainer's TF32 WMMA forward is restricted to `d_h ∈ {16, 32}` due to shared memory constraints. [S5] The FP32 WMMA forward kernel requires `kDh % 8 == 0` for TF32 WMMA path, but the shared memory footprint at `kDh=64` (~96 KB) exceeds the 48 KB static cap, necessitating dynamic shared memory opt-in, which is not used in the scalar fallback path for this dimension in the FP32

The daemon decides which model loader to call based on the checkpoint's schema version: v3 triggers `CudaMlp::Load`, v5 triggers `CudaTransformer::Load`, and any other version returns a 404 with a stderr line [S2]. The number of loaded checkpoints kept resident on the GPU is bounded by the `--model-cache-size` flag, which defaults to 2 and caps the cache under LRU eviction [S1][S3][S4].

The daemon decides which model loader to call based on the checkpoint's schema version: v3 triggers `CudaMlp::Load`, v5 triggers `CudaTransformer::Load`, and any other version results in a 404 response with a stderr log entry [S2][S5]. The number of loaded checkpoints kept resident on the GPU is bounded by the `--model-cache-size` flag, which defaults to 2 and uses LRU eviction [S1][S3][S4].

The daemon decides which model loader to call based on the checkpoint's schema version: v3 triggers `CudaMlp::Load`, v5 triggers `CudaTransformer::Load`, and any other version returns a 404 with a stderr line. [S7] The number of loaded checkpoints kept resident on the GPU is bounded by the `--model-cache-size` flag, which defaults to 2 and enforces LRU eviction. [S2]

The README flags entrypoint-level concerns about model input/output widths (kInputSize, kOutputSize) and history handling (RingBufferView) when retargeting to a new game, and specifies that the template layer under include/az/** and src/az/** is supposed to stay untouched. [S1] [S2] [S4] [S8]

The daemon decides which model loader to call based on the checkpoint's schema version: v3 triggers `CudaMlp::Load`, v5 triggers `CudaTransformer::Load`, and any other version results in a 404 response [S2]. The number of loaded checkpoints kept resident on the GPU is bounded by the `--model-cache-size` option, which defaults to 2 and uses LRU eviction [S1][S4][S5].

The README flags that entrypoints need to adjust model input/output widths (`kInputSize`, `kOutputSize`) to match the new game's serializer output and handle history by wiring up a real `RingBufferView` if `kHistoryLookback > 0` [S1]. The template layer under `include/az/` and `src/az/` is supposed to stay untouched [S1].

The README flags that entrypoints must adjust model input/output widths (`kInputSize`, `kOutputSize`) to match the new game's serializer output and handle history correctly if `kHistoryLookback > 0` by wiring up a real history window instead of passing an empty `RingBufferView`. The template layer under `include/az/` and `src/az/` is supposed to stay untouched, as it is game-agnostic. [S1] [S7]

The README flags that entrypoints hardcode the XQ type in `src/arena.cc`, `src/mcts.cc`, and `src/train.cc`, requiring updates to includes and type usage, and that model dimensions (`kInputSize`, `kOutputSize`) in `src/arena.cc` and `src/train.cc` must be sized to the new game's serializer output, and that if the new game's `kHistoryLookback > 0`, the empty `RingBufferView` currently passed to the serializer becomes incorrect and must be wired up per `docs/alpha_zero_improvement.md`. The template layer under `include/az/` and `src/az/` is supposed to stay untouched. [S1]

The checkpoint writer currently emits the V5 schema, which appends two float material targets (`material_own`, `material_opp`) per row over the previous V4 layout. [S2]  
The markdown describing this format is out of date in `src/az/train/replay-checkpoint.md`, which states the writer always emits the compact V4 row layout. [S3]

The checkpoint writer currently emits the V5 schema, which appends two float material targets (`material_own`, `material_opp`) per row over the previous V4 layout. [S2] The markdown that describes this format out of date is `src/az/train/replay-checkpoint.md`, which states the writer always emits the compact V4 row layout. [S3]

The GUI server reconstructs the per-artifact directory layout by reading the `[storage]` table from `run.toml` in the resolved config directory and resolving each path against `storage.root_dir` via `readStorageLayout`, mirroring the C++ trainer's `ResolveStorageDir` helper to ensure matching resolution [S7][S8]. The environment variables `AZ_TRAINING_DIR` (explicit pin) and `AZ_CONFIG_ROOT` (auto-pick newest `run.toml` one level deep) decide whether the GUI pins to one run or auto-picks the newest one [S1][S2][S3][S4][S5][S6].

The GUI server reconstructs the per-artifact directory layout by reading `run.toml`'s `[storage]` table and resolving each path against `storage.root_dir` via `readStorageLayout`, mirroring the C++ trainer's resolution so both use the same layout [S1][S2][S3][S6][S7][S8][S12][S13][S14][S15][S17][S18]. The environment variables `AZ_TRAINING_DIR` (explicit pin) and `AZ_CONFIG_ROOT` (auto-pick newest `run.toml` one level deep) decide whether the GUI pins to one run or auto-picks the newest one [S1][S2][S3][S4][S5][S6][S7][S8][S12][S13][S15].

The policy head is left in FP16 because cuBLASLt's sm_120 FP8 algorithm catalog rejects unaligned N dimensions, and the head GEMM's output_size (105 for compact Xiang Qi) is not a multiple of 16. [S1] [S2] [S7] [S10]

The GUI server reconstructs the per-artifact directory layout by reading `run.toml` from the active config directory, joining `storage.root_dir` onto each non-absolute per-artifact path from the `[storage]` table, and spawning the daemon with `--checkpoint-dir` and `--games-dir` set to the resolved `storage.model_checkpoint_dir` and `storage.gui_game_trace_dir` [S1][S2][S5][S6][S7]. The environment variables `AZ_TRAINING_DIR` and `AZ_CONFIG_ROOT` decide whether the GUI pins to one run or auto-picks the newest one: `AZ_TRAINING_DIR` explicitly pins the GUI to a config directory, while `AZ_CONFIG_ROOT` is used as the enumeration root when `AZ_TRAINING_DIR` is unset, causing the GUI to pick the newest `run.toml` one level deep under that root [S1][S2][S5][S6][S7].

The GUI server reconstructs the per-artifact directory layout by resolving the active config directory from `AZ_TRAINING_DIR` (explicit pin) or `AZ_CONFIG_ROOT` (auto-pick of the newest `run.toml` one level deep), then reading `run.toml`'s `[storage]` table and resolving each per-artifact path against `storage.root_dir` via `readStorageLayout`, mirroring the C++ trainer's resolution so the GUI and daemon use identical paths. [S1][S2][S3][S4][S5][S6][S7][S8] The environment variables that decide whether the GUI pins to one run or auto-picks the newest one are `AZ_TRAINING_DIR` (for explicit pin) and `AZ_CONFIG_ROOT` (for auto-pick of the newest `run.toml` under the root). [S1][S2][S3][S4][S5][S6]

The checkpoint writer emits V5 schema today, which appends two float material targets (`material_own`, `material_opp`) per row over the previous V3 layout that added `opp_legal_indices`, `opp_target_values`, and `opp_present` after the ply field. [S2] [S3] [S6] [S7] [S10]  
The markdown that describes this format out of date is `src/az/train/replay-checkpoint.md`, which states the writer always emits the compact V4 row layout (`kSchemaCompactV4 = 4`). [S4]

The policy head is left in FP16 because its output size (105 for compact Xiang Qi) does not satisfy the cuBLASLt FP8 GEMM shape requirement that M, N, and K must all be multiples of 16, so the FP8 replica keeps W_head in FP16 and routes the head through the existing FP16 GEMM. [S6]

The checkpoint writer currently emits the V4 compact replay buffer schema, which includes the fields: input[input_dim], value, legal_indices[k_dim], target_values[k_dim], push_step, ply, opp_legal_indices[k_dim], opp_target_values[k_dim], opp_present, and state_bytes[state_bytes_dim] (zero-padded when state_bytes_dim == 0). [S6]  
Over the previous V3 layout, the writer appends the state_bytes[state_bytes_dim] field per row to support reanalyze worker state snapshots. [S6]  
The markdown that describes this format out of date is docs/replay_buffer_checkpoint.md, as it states the current schema is V4 but the writer now emits V5 due to the material_head addition in CompactTrainingSample, which is not reflected in the document. [S1][S6]

The policy head is left in FP16 because the head GEMM has shape (M=B, N=output_size, K=d_model) and output_size is not generally a multiple of 16, causing cuBLASLt's FP8 algorithm catalog to reject unaligned N with NOT_SUPPORTED. [S1] [S2] [S4] [S5] [S17] [S19] [S20] [S7] [S15] [S13] [S3] [S12] [S14] [S16] [S18] [S6] [S8] [S9] [S10] [S11]

Values are stored and propagated in the leaf's last_player_'s perspective, and a network value or an ancestor's contribution is sign-flipped during backup if the last_player_ differs from the current player (cur_player_) at that node. [S5]

Values are stored and propagated in the leaf's `last_player_`'s perspective, meaning the player who just moved into that state, not the player about to move. [S1] During backup, a network value is negated if `last_player_ != cur_player_` for non-terminal leaves. [S1] An ancestor's contribution is sign-flipped if its `LastPlayer()` differs from the leaf's `last_player_`. [S1]

A network value is negated during backup if `last_player_ != cur_player_` for non-terminal leaves. [S1]  
An ancestor's contribution is sign-flipped during backup if its `LastPlayer()` differs from the leaf's `last_player_`. [S1]

The reanalyze worker uses the `SnapshotForReanalyze` API to obtain slot generations (push ids) for the rows it samples. [S2] It replaces only the `legal_indices` and `target_values` fields of the replay row, while carrying over unchanged the `input`, `value`, `push_step`, `ply`, `opp_*`, `state_bytes`, and `material_*` fields from the original row. [S3] [S4]

The sampling API that provides slot generations is `CompactReplayBuffer::SnapshotForReanalyze`, which returns per-slot push ids (generations) along with slot indices and rows [S2]. The reanalyze worker replaces only `legal_indices` and `target_values` in the replay row, while carrying over unchanged `input`, `value`, `push_step`, `ply`, `opp_*`, `state_bytes`, and `material_*` from the original row [S3][S4].

The reanalyze worker uses the `SnapshotForReanalyze` API to obtain slot generations (push ids) for the rows it samples. [S2] It replaces the `legal_indices` and `target_values` fields of the replay row with newly computed MCTS results, while carrying over unchanged the `input`, `value`, `push_step`, `ply`, `opp_*`, `state_bytes`, and `material_*` fields from the original row. [S4][S5]

The reanalyze worker uses the `SnapshotForReanalyze` API to obtain slot generations (push IDs) for sampled rows. [S5] It replaces the `legal_indices` and `target_values` fields of the replay row with freshly computed MCTS results, while carrying over unchanged the `input`, `value`, `push_step`, `ply`, `opp_legal_indices`, `opp_target_values`, `opp_present`, `state_bytes`, `material_own`, and `material_opp` fields from the original row. [S11] [S12]

The training-time symmetry augmenter must avoid returning `target` unchanged, as this trains the network to be augmentation-invariant rather than equivariant. [S1]  
The scalar value target `target.z` must be preserved unchanged under symmetry, as board symmetries are score-preserving. [S1]

The policy head is left in FP16 because the head GEMM has shape (M=B, N=output_size, K=d_model) and output_size is set by the game (e.g. 1+8100 for Xiang Qi), which is not a multiple of 16, causing cuBLASLt's FP8 algorithm catalog to reject the N alignment with NOT_SUPPORTED. [S3] The head stays FP16 because output_size (105 for compact Xiang Qi) is not generally a multiple of 16, and cuBLASLt's sm_120 FP8 algorithm catalog rejects unaligned N. [S1] The head GEMM (M=B, N=output_size, K=d_model) does not satisfy the multiple-of-16 requirement in general (output_size = 105 for compact Xiang Qi), so the FP8 replica keeps W_head in FP16 and routes the head through the existing FP16 GEMM. [S8] Body GEMMs dominate the inference cost, so keeping the head in higher precision has minimal impact on performance. [S3] [S8]

The training-time symmetry augmenter must avoid returning the target unchanged, as that trains the network to be augmentation-invariant rather than equivariant. The scalar value target (z) must be preserved unchanged under symmetry. [S9]

The documented bug is that returning `target` unchanged trains the network to be augmentation-invariant rather than equivariant, which is wrong. [S1]  
The scalar value target `target.z` must be preserved unchanged under symmetry. [S1]

The in-memory model cache is keyed by the sha256 of the checkpoint file, and if the file's mtime changes, the cache entry is evicted and reloaded on the next /infer request. Cache capacity is configurable via --model-cache-size (default 2) and uses LRU eviction. Concurrency is controlled by a per-cache-entry mutex: each /infer request grabs the mutex for the model's worker thread, serializing concurrent requests against the same model while allowing interleaving across different models. The /models endpoint reports for each checkpoint: path, sha256, mtime, size_bytes, and schema_version. [S6][S5]

The board has only left/right mirror symmetry — the river divides the two sides asymmetrically and the palaces / pieces are tied to specific files. Rotations break the river / palaces; vertical flip would swap players (a perspective change, not a symmetry). [S1]

The board has only left/right mirror symmetry — the river divides the two sides asymmetrically and the palaces / pieces are tied to specific files. Rotations break the river / palaces; vertical flip would swap players (a perspective change, not a symmetry). [S1]

The training-time symmetry augmenter must avoid returning `target` unchanged, as this trains the network to be augmentation-invariant rather than equivariant, which is wrong. [S1] The scalar value target `target.z` must be preserved unchanged under symmetry, as board symmetries are score-preserving. [S1] [S4] [S5] [S10]

The single board symmetry exploited for training is the left/right mirror across the central file (column 4). [S3]  
Rotations and vertical flips are excluded because they are not symmetries of the game due to the river dividing the two sides asymmetrically and the palaces being tied to specific files. [S3]

The board has only left/right mirror symmetry — the river divides the two sides asymmetrically and the palaces / pieces are tied to specific files. Rotations break the river / palaces; vertical flip would swap players (a perspective change, not a symmetry). [S1]

Graphify's coding-session provenance was the blocking issue: in the final inception history graph, the nodes' `source_file` values pointed at files merely mentioned inside the transcripts (such as `.agents/skills/...`) rather than the transcript file that actually contained the evidence, so all three inception history queries missed their primary session file. [S4]

The blocking provenance defect in graphify was that its inception history graph pointed many nodes at files mentioned inside transcripts instead of the transcript files containing the evidence, causing all three inception history queries to miss their primary session file. [S5] The graph node field that exhibited this defect was the source field, as nodes were incorrectly attributed to mentioned files rather than the actual transcript source. [S5]

The in-memory model cache is keyed by the sha256 of the checkpoint file, as stated in the model cache documentation: "A single `unordered_map<string, shared_ptr<IModel>>` keyed by the sha256 of the checkpoint file." [S1] Concurrency is controlled via a per-model mutex (`search_mu`) that serializes `/infer` requests against the same model, while allowing concurrent requests against different models to interleave: "Each `/infer` request grabs the per-model worker by mutex and runs MCTS inline. Concurrent requests against the same model are serialized; concurrent requests against different models can interleave." [S1] The model-listing endpoint (`/models`) reports for each checkpoint the fields `path`, `sha256`, `mtime_unix`, `size_bytes`, and `schema_version` (referred to as `version` in the code), as seen in the response construction: `models.push_back(json{{"path", e.path}, {"sha256", e.sha256}, {"mtime_unix", e.mtime_unix}, {"size_bytes", e.size_bytes}, {"schema_version", e.version}});` [S2] and defined in the `ListEntry` struct: `std::string path; std::string sha256; std::time_t mtime_unix = 0; uint64_t size_bytes = 0; uint32_t version = 0;` [S3] [S5].

Graphify's coding-session provenance was the blocking issue: the final inception history graph had nodes whose `source_file` values were files mentioned by transcripts, such as `.agents/skills/...`, instead of the transcript file that contained the evidence. [S1] This made graphify poor at answering "which session recorded this?" unless the extraction prompt or graph schema preserves both "evidence file" and "mentioned file". [S1] The defect was in the `source_file` field of graph nodes. [S1]

[S3]

The core hypothesis is called the **coupling thesis** [S16] and the statistic that captures it in the 2×2 factorial is the **interaction term** [S3]. The control added because the corpus is this repo is the **closed-book parametric-leakage control** [S6].

The core hypothesis is named the coupling thesis [S1]. The statistic in the 2x2 factorial that captures it is the interaction term (Dr−Db)−(Nr−Nb) [S2]. The control added because the corpus is this very repo is the frozen retrieval gate [S3].

The local GraphRAG prototype achieved the strongest recall across both domains and projects with no LLM indexing tokens, reaching 1.000 recall@20 on both history and code corpora for inception and alpha-zero. [S3] The practical decision was not to replace setting-up-rag with graphify, as graphify needs provenance fixes and better code query seeding before it can be trusted for context-retrieval skills. [S1] [S12]

gpt-4.1-mini is the default OpenAI model for shirt thoughts. The first provider to return valid parsed text wins the race, and a fast error or empty response does not win. [S1][S2][S4]

The default OpenAI model chosen was gpt-4.1-mini. The subtle rule that decided which provider's result wins the race is using the first successful parsed thought, not the first network response, so a fast error or empty provider response does not win. [S4]
1024-token chunks, 10% overlap, Porter/stemming tokenizer [S3]

The default OpenAI model chosen for the shirt-thought race is `gpt-4.1-mini` [S1][S6]. The subtle rule that decides which provider's result wins the race is that the first provider to return valid parsed text wins, not the first network response, so a fast error or empty provider response does not win [S1][S2].

The default OpenAI model chosen for the shirt-thought racer is `gpt-4.1-mini` [S1][S4][S12]. The subtle rule that decides which provider's result wins the race is that the first provider to return valid parsed text wins, and a fast error, empty response, or provider-level abort does not win; the Worker waits for the other provider before failing the whole request [S2][S18].

The concrete defect verified on the machine is that a `jj workspace add` working copy has `.jj` but **no `.git`**, causing `git status` to fail with "not a git repository" [S2]. The rule is that plain Enter prevents the default newline and calls onSubmit, while Shift/Alt/Meta/Ctrl Enter and IME composition still avoid submission. [S2] The mobile interaction wired to submit is blur on mobile/coarse-pointer layouts. [S2]
